name: Chandrahas Sword
description: The Chandrahas Sword, also known as Chandrahasa, is a legendary sword of immense power in Hindu mythology, primarily associated with Ravana, the ten-headed demon king of Lanka. Gifted to Ravana by Lord Shiva himself, the sword is described as being as radiant as the moon and possessing unparalleled sharpness and destructive capabilities. Its name, Chandrahas, literally translates to 'laughter of the moon' or 'moon's smile,' reflecting its ethereal beauty and terrifying potential. Ravana wielded the Chandrahas with great skill and ferocity, using it to conquer vast territories and challenge even the gods. The sword's power was so great that it granted Ravana near-invincibility, making him a formidable opponent. However, the Chandrahas also represented Ravana's arrogance and misuse of divine power, ultimately contributing to his downfall at the hands of Lord Rama. The sword is not merely a weapon but a symbol of power, devotion, and the consequences of unchecked ambition. Its possession signifies a direct link to divine energy, but also a heavy responsibility to wield it righteously. The tale of Chandrahas serves as a cautionary narrative about the balance between power and dharma (righteousness). Legend dictates that the sword returns to Lord Shiva upon the death of its wielder, suggesting the ultimate source of its power and its intended purpose within the cosmic order. The sword's existence highlights the complex interplay between gods, demons, and mortals in the Hindu epics.
subtitle: The Moon-Laughter Sword of Ravana
origin: Gifted to Ravana by Lord Shiva
currentLocation: Returned to Lord Shiva after Ravana's death (spiritual realm)

keyMyths.title: Ravana's Acquisition
keyMyths.description: Ravana propitiated Lord Shiva with intense devotion and penance. Pleased with his devotion, Shiva gifted him the Chandrahas sword as a boon.

keyMyths.title: Ravana's Downfall
keyMyths.description: Despite its immense power, Chandrahas could not save Ravana from Rama's righteous arrow. The sword's power was ultimately subservient to dharma.

sources.author: Valmiki
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Rama and Ravana.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Rama and Ravana.
